focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 13

Gavin hadn't slept. After the notation blurred into shapes on the page, he'd shoved the manuscript back into his trunk and climbed until the castle ran out of stairs. The Astronomy Tower was the coldest place in the castle, and he hadn't brought a cloak.

The stone floor leached heat through his robes as he leaned back against the parapet and pulled the cloth tight around his chest. Wind came through the gaps between the crenellations in bursts that flattened his hair and then died and came again. He'd been up here for a while. An hour, maybe. Long enough that his hands had stopped just being cold and had moved into the numb stage where the fingers didn't bend right when he tried to make a fist.

March in Scotland. The thaw had started but hadn't committed — patches of snow still on the mountains, the grounds mostly mud with green pushing through in places, the lake half-frozen and half-not in a way that looked like a decision the water couldn't make. From up here the Forbidden Forest was a dark shape pressed against the hills, and below it the lawns sloped down to Hagrid's cabin, which was dark. Hagrid might be in the forest tonight. Gavin didn't know. He hadn't paid attention to Hagrid's schedule in months. He hadn't paid attention to most things in months.

He'd come up because the sub-collection was too small. The walls of those cut-stone rooms had started to press. Fourteen weeks of working there — the limestone smell in his clothes, the faint glow of stones that predated everything else in the castle — and the air had begun to feel used. He'd kept walking out of the dormitory, out of the common room, up the stairs past the library landing, then up more stairs past the Dark Arts classroom, dark now with the door closed, and up again. He'd ended at the...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...ted. The wall sconce opposite his bed thinned from a steady amber tongue to a blue needle edged in orange. The change was small, maybe two seconds, but the whole dormitory felt narrower in that interval, as if the stone had drawn half an inch closer to his ribs. On the bed the altered quill lost another degree of itself. The shaft did not just blur. Its middle seemed to go missing, nib and vane hanging in the air with barely enough quill between them.

Then the pressure eased. The sconce swelled back to amber. The window went still. The quill settled into its usual half-seen state on the blanket.

Gavin wiped his nose and looked at the quill. The wards had thinned again. It was not enough for anyone else in Hufflepuff to wake up and say the room felt wrong. It was not enough to leave a mark you could point at tomorrow morning. But the room's magic had registered it, and the first thing he'd changed had registered it worst of all.

If the altered quill reacted to ward strain, then it was not only proof he'd managed one edit. It was a gauge. Crude and stupid and built out of a split school quill, but still a gauge.

He cleaned the handkerchief with a Scourgify that mostly worked — his wand still did ordinary spells, and he was still mediocre at them — and the worst of the blood vanished, leaving a faint pinkish ghost on the cotton. Then he set the altered quill on the nightstand instead of putting it back in his pocket. After a moment he opened his trunk, found another cheap school quill, and laid it beside the first.

After lessons tomorrow he'd take both back to the sub-collection, where the castle's strain was easiest to feel. If the altered one went thin and the ordinary one didn't, then the wards were touching the loosened agreement directly. He could work with that.
